text: THE STATEâ€™S LEGAL RESPONSIBILITY TOWARDS ABDUCTION OF MINORS IN INDONESIA This study is aimed at scrutinizing the Stateâ€™s legal and operational responsibility towards the protection of abandoned children in Indonesia as outlined in Article 34 paragraph (1) of the 1945 Constitution of the Republic of Indonesia. Despite the existence of robust legislation â€“ the International Convention on the Rights of the Child (ICRC) and Law No. 23 of 2002 on Child Protection â€“ the number of abandoned children in Indonesia is consistently increasing which indicates that there is a huge disparity between constitutional principle and administrative apparatus. The method used in this research is normative legal research with a conceptual and statutory approach. Analysis and discussion of the studies show that there are several obstacles faced by the State in protecting abandoned children including: - Not yet reaching the concept of good governance in the government apparatus - The government does not have enough strong will in overcoming the phenomenon of abandoned children - The State does not have enough budget to conduct social protection for children, therefore most of the social protection is only focused on the education and health for children; - Other secondary obstacles are: - The apparatus of the State Law Enforcement Agency (Police) which has not maximized the role and function in protecting abandoned children; - The community and parent who do not yet know about the rights of children; - The absence of cooperation between the apparatus of the State and non government organizations that protect children. Therefore, the State has to make a change by carrying out a multi-faceted governance, by increasing the State budget for the education and health given to children, and to synchronize laws with international agreements so as to support the implementation of child protection effectively.
explanation: Reasonable likelihood of AI involvement given the formal, generic legal-analytic style, but the text contains factual inconsistencies (ICRC vs CRC) and encoding artifacts that often appear in mixed human/Ai or OCR-generated text.
